title: Operators as Differentiable STRIPS
subtitle: The neuro-symbolic bridge ‚Äî from classical AI planning to learned semantic operators

children.children: STRIPS gave AI planning its foundation: actions with preconditions and effects. Schankian operators are the neural equivalent ‚Äî learned, soft, compositional. This tutorial bridges classical AI planning and modern deep learning through the lens of semantic operators.

In 1971, Fikes and Nilsson created STRIPS (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver) ‚Äî a formalism where every action has three parts: preconditions (what must be true), add effects (what becomes true), and delete effects (what stops being true).

props.title: üß± Classical STRIPS Action Builder

children.children: Notice TEACH has no delete effects ‚Äî the teacher doesn't lose knowledge by sharing it. GIVE deletes from donor and adds to recipient. This asymmetry is exactly what Schank captured: ATRANS (possession transfer) vs MTRANS (information transfer) are fundamentally different operator types.

STRIPS is elegant but brittle. Every action, precondition, and effect must be hand-specified as crisp logical propositions. Real language is messy: 'She slid the book across the table to him' involves PTRANS + ATRANS, but no STRIPS planner would figure that out without someone explicitly encoding it.

items.definition: How do formal symbols like 'on(A,B)' connect to actual perception and language? STRIPS assumes someone already solved this.
With N predicates and M objects, the state space grows exponentially. Planning becomes intractable for realistic domains.
Anything not stated is false. But in natural language, most information is implicit ‚Äî the Frame Problem in disguise.
A STRIPS planner for cooking can't help with chemistry, even though both involve COMBINE and SEPARATE operators.

children.children: STRIPS is like IKEA instructions: every step precisely specified, works perfectly IF you have exactly the right pieces, but completely useless if your situation deviates even slightly. Neural operators are like an experienced carpenter ‚Äî they understand the PRINCIPLES of assembly and can improvise.

props.title: 3. The Neural Upgrade: Preconditions as Regions, Effects as Rotations

children.children: Here's the key insight: replace every crisp component of STRIPS with a soft, learned equivalent. Preconditions become regions in embedding space. Effects become geometric transformations (rotations). The result: operators that work on real language, generalize across domains, and compose naturally.

props.title: üîÑ Symbolic ‚Üí Neural Translation

children.children: The delete-effects translation is particularly elegant: rotations naturally handle adds and deletes in one operation. Dimensions that rotate 'toward' a value are adds; dimensions that rotate 'away' are deletes. The frame problem ‚Äî what doesn't change ‚Äî is solved by default: unrotated dimensions are preserved automatically.

props.title: 4. The Frame Problem: STRIPS' Achilles Heel, Operators' Free Lunch

children.children: The Frame Problem asks: when an action occurs, what DOESN'T change? In STRIPS, you must explicitly list every unchanged fact (frame axioms). With N predicates, this means O(N) frame axioms per action. In neural operators, unchanged dimensions are preserved by default ‚Äî rotation only affects the subspace it acts on.

props.title: üñºÔ∏è Frame Problem Comparison
Why rotations solve the frame problem

children.children: A rotation matrix R in SO(d) can be decomposed into block-diagonal form: some 2√ó2 blocks rotate (these are the effects), and the remaining dimensions get identity blocks (these are the frame axioms ‚Äî for free). The rank of (R - I) tells you how many dimensions the operator actually affects. A good operator should have low rank: it changes only what it needs to.

This connects to Entry #12 (Disentangled Operator Dimensions): if each dimension encodes an independent semantic property, then a sparse rotation means the operator only touches the relevant properties. Polarity, volitionality, completion ‚Äî each gets its own dimension, and most operators only rotate a few.

props.title: 5. Composition: Where Neural Operators Really Shine

children.children: In STRIPS, composing actions means chaining: apply action 1, check preconditions for action 2, apply it. In neural operators, composition is matrix multiplication: R_combined = R‚ÇÇ ¬∑ R‚ÇÅ. This enables gradient-based plan optimization ‚Äî you can differentiate through the entire plan.

props.title: üîó Operator Composition Lab

children.children: Positioning operators as 'differentiable STRIPS' connects three traditionally separate communities, each of which has something the others need.

props.title: üåê Community Bridge Explorer

children.children: Putting it all together, here's the architecture: operators as a differentiable action language that combines the precision of STRIPS with the flexibility of neural networks.
Input: 'The thief snatched the old woman's purse and ran.'
Step 1 ‚Äî Parse participants: thief (agent), old woman (source), purse (object)
Step 2 ‚Äî Embed: [e_thief ‚äï e_woman ‚äï e_purse] ‚Üí state vector s‚ÇÄ
Step 3 ‚Äî Classify operator: context ‚Üí ATRANS (forced possession transfer)
Step 4 ‚Äî Apply: s‚ÇÅ = R_ATRANS ¬∑ s‚ÇÄ
Step 5 ‚Äî Decode state change: thief.has(purse)=true, woman.has(purse)=false
Step 6 ‚Äî Implicit states: woman.distress=high, thief.fleeing=true (inferred by operator!)

The implicit states in Step 6 are the magic. STRIPS would need explicit rules for every emotional consequence. Neural operators LEARN these from data ‚Äî the rotation naturally encodes that forced ATRANS (theft) produces distress in the source, because that pattern appears consistently in training narratives.

questions.question: What are the three components of a STRIPS action?
questions.explanation: STRIPS actions have preconditions (what must be true), add effects (what becomes true), and delete effects (what ceases to be true).

questions.question: How do neural operators solve the frame problem?
options: By listing all unchanged facts explicitly
By using rotation matrices that preserve unaffected dimensions automatically
questions.explanation: Rotation matrices only affect the subspace they act on. Unrotated dimensions are preserved by the identity ‚Äî the frame problem is solved by the geometry itself.

questions.question: Why is MTRANS (information transfer) fundamentally different from ATRANS (possession transfer)?
options: MTRANS has no delete effects ‚Äî the source keeps what they share
questions.explanation: When you teach someone, you don't lose the knowledge (no delete effect). When you give someone an object, you no longer have it (delete effect). This is a fundamental structural difference captured by different operator geometries.

questions.question: What makes operator composition superior to STRIPS action chaining?
options: It's fully differentiable ‚Äî you can backpropagate through the entire plan
questions.explanation: Matrix multiplication is differentiable, so you can optimize plan structure via gradient descent. STRIPS chaining requires discrete search through state space.

items.definition: Operators extract what LLMs already know about action effects ‚Äî we're not teaching, we're organizing.
SRL tells you who-did-what-to-whom. Operators add: and here's what CHANGED.
Operators as the LLVM of language ‚Äî an intermediate representation between surface text and world-state computation.
Scripts are reward machines; operators are the transition functions. Bridges event semantics and reinforcement learning.
The Event Calculus's Initiates/Terminates predicates are exactly our operators ‚Äî learned geometrically.
